cases.title: Prostate needle biopsy: acinar adenocarcinoma, Grade Group 1
cases.specimenType: Left apex prostate needle core biopsy
cases.clinicalHistory: 68-year-old man with PSA 6.4 ng/mL and PI-RADS 4 lesion in the left peripheral zone.

image.caption: Acinar prostatic adenocarcinoma on H&E stain.

diagnosticSteps: Identify infiltrative small glands with nuclear enlargement and prominent nucleoli.
Confirm absence of basal cells if morphology is limited or potentially mimicked by benign glands.
Report Gleason score, Grade Group, number of involved cores, linear extent, and percent involvement.

ancillaryOptions.name: PIN4 or AMACR plus basal-cell cocktail
ancillaryOptions.result: AMACR positive with absent basal-cell staining in malignant glands.

ancillaryOptions.name: NKX3.1
ancillaryOptions.result: Nuclear positivity supports prostatic origin.

ancillaryOptions.name: ERG
ancillaryOptions.result: ERG is not required for routine grading.

cases.reportingTarget: Prostate, left apex, needle core biopsy: Acinar adenocarcinoma, Gleason score 3+3=6, Grade Group 1, involving 1 of 2 cores, 3 mm linear extent, 20% of submitted tissue.
pitfalls: Do not omit core count or linear extent.
Do not use immunostains to grade the tumor.

cases.title: Prostate biopsy: acinar adenocarcinoma with Gleason pattern 4
cases.clinicalHistory: 72-year-old man with rising PSA and MRI-targeted biopsy from a right posterior peripheral-zone lesion.

image.caption: Prostatic adenocarcinoma with Gleason pattern 4 architecture.

diagnosticSteps: Recognize fused, poorly formed, or glomeruloid glands as Gleason pattern 4.
Estimate the percent pattern 4 because it changes risk communication.
Report Gleason score, Grade Group, core involvement, and cribriform/intraductal carcinoma if present.

ancillaryOptions.name: Basal-cell markers plus AMACR
ancillaryOptions.result: May support carcinoma in a limited focus but does not assign grade.

ancillaryOptions.name: PTEN
ancillaryOptions.result: Not required for routine sign-out.

ancillaryOptions.name: No ancillary study
ancillaryOptions.result: Morphology is sufficient for grading in this case.

cases.reportingTarget: Prostate, targeted core biopsy: Acinar adenocarcinoma, Gleason score 3+4=7, Grade Group 2, pattern 4 approximately 20%, involving 2 of 3 cores, greatest linear extent 7 mm.
pitfalls: Do not bury percent pattern 4.
Do not call all small glands pattern 3 when fused architecture is present.

cases.title: Prostate chips: benign prostatic hyperplasia
cases.clinicalHistory: 76-year-old man with bladder outlet obstruction and no suspicious lesion on preoperative evaluation.

image.caption: Benign prostatic hyperplasia with nodular glandular and stromal proliferation.

diagnosticSteps: Confirm benign nodular glandular and stromal proliferation.
Search for incidental carcinoma when sampling volume or gross concern warrants microscopy.
Report inflammation, infarct, or incidental carcinoma only if present.

ancillaryOptions.name: No ancillary study
ancillaryOptions.result: No ancillary study is required for uncomplicated benign nodular hyperplasia.

ancillaryOptions.name: Basal-cell markers plus AMACR
ancillaryOptions.result: Reserve for a suspicious microscopic focus.

cases.reportingTarget: Prostate, transurethral resection: Benign prostatic tissue with nodular glandular and stromal hyperplasia.
pitfalls: Do not sign out gross-only if tissue has not been examined per local sampling policy.
Do not ignore a firm or yellow gross focus.

cases.title: Bladder biopsy: urothelial carcinoma in situ
cases.clinicalHistory: 69-year-old man with positive urine cytology and erythematous posterior bladder wall mucosa.

image.caption: Urothelial carcinoma in situ with high-grade cytologic atypia.

diagnosticSteps: Recognize flat high-grade cytologic atypia involving urothelium.
Assess whether lamina propria invasion is present.
State muscularis propria presence or absence when applicable.

ancillaryOptions.name: CK20 and p53
ancillaryOptions.result: Diffuse CK20 and aberrant p53 support CIS in the correct morphology.

ancillaryOptions.name: GMS
ancillaryOptions.result: No fungal organisms identified.

ancillaryOptions.name: Ki-67
ancillaryOptions.result: Expanded proliferation supports but does not replace morphology.

cases.reportingTarget: Urinary bladder, posterior wall, biopsy: Urothelial carcinoma in situ. No lamina propria invasion identified. Muscularis propria is not present.
pitfalls: Do not call reactive atypia CIS without full cytologic and architectural support.
Always state muscularis propria status when relevant.

cases.title: TURBT: papillary urothelial neoplasm of low malignant potential
cases.clinicalHistory: 61-year-old man with a small papillary bladder tumor near the left lateral wall.

image.caption: Papillary urothelial neoplasm of low malignant potential, low-power H&E.

diagnosticSteps: Identify orderly papillary fronds with increased urothelial thickness but minimal cytologic atypia.
Exclude low-grade papillary urothelial carcinoma if architectural disorder and cytologic atypia are greater.
Assess lamina propria invasion and muscularis propria presence.

ancillaryOptions.name: No ancillary study
ancillaryOptions.result: Papillary urothelial neoplasia grading is morphology based.

ancillaryOptions.name: CK20 and p53
ancillaryOptions.result: Not required for routine PUNLMP diagnosis.

cases.reportingTarget: Urinary bladder, tumor, transurethral resection: Papillary urothelial neoplasm of low malignant potential. No invasion identified. Muscularis propria is present and uninvolved.
requiredReportElements: papillary urothelial neoplasm of low malignant potential
pitfalls: Do not omit muscularis propria status.
Do not overgrade a bland orderly papillary lesion.

cases.title: TURBT: high-grade papillary urothelial carcinoma
cases.clinicalHistory: 74-year-old man with a 3.1 cm papillary bladder tumor and hematuria.

image.caption: High-grade papillary urothelial carcinoma on H&E stain.

diagnosticSteps: Recognize disordered papillary architecture with high-grade cytologic atypia.
Determine whether lamina propria invasion is present.
State muscularis propria presence and involvement because staging adequacy depends on it.

ancillaryOptions.name: Desmin or smoothelin
ancillaryOptions.result: May help separate muscularis mucosae from muscularis propria in difficult cases.

ancillaryOptions.name: GATA3
ancillaryOptions.result: Supports urothelial lineage but is not needed for a typical primary bladder tumor.

cases.reportingTarget: Urinary bladder, tumor, transurethral resection: High-grade papillary urothelial carcinoma with lamina propria invasion. Muscularis propria is present and uninvolved.
requiredReportElements: muscularis propria is present and uninvolved
pitfalls: Do not report stage without muscularis propria status.
Do not ignore cautery-limited invasion assessment.

cases.title: Bladder biopsy after BCG: granulomatous cystitis
cases.clinicalHistory: 70-year-old man with prior high-grade noninvasive urothelial carcinoma treated with intravesical BCG, now with erythematous mucosa.
diagnosticSteps: Recognize granulomatous inflammation in the post-BCG setting.
Search the urothelium for residual or recurrent carcinoma in situ.
Use organism stains when necrosis, organisms, or clinical infection concern is present.

ancillaryOptions.name: AFB stain
ancillaryOptions.result: No acid-fast bacilli identified.

ancillaryOptions.name: GMS stain
ancillaryOptions.result: No fungal organisms identified.

ancillaryOptions.name: CK20 and p53
ancillaryOptions.result: Reserve for urothelial atypia suspicious for CIS.

cases.reportingTarget: Urinary bladder, biopsy: Granulomatous cystitis consistent with treatment effect in the setting of prior intravesical BCG. No carcinoma identified.
pitfalls: Do not miss recurrent CIS in adjacent urothelium.
Do not diagnose infection without organism or clinical support.

cases.title: Nephrectomy: clear cell renal cell carcinoma
cases.clinicalHistory: 64-year-old man with a 5.8 cm enhancing upper-pole renal mass.

image.caption: Clear cell renal cell carcinoma morphology.

diagnosticSteps: Identify clear cells with delicate vasculature compatible with clear cell RCC.
Assign WHO/ISUP nucleolar grade and document necrosis or sarcomatoid/rhabdoid features if present.
Complete resection fields: size, extent, margins, renal sinus/perinephric fat, vein, nodes, and pT category.

ancillaryOptions.name: CAIX and PAX8
ancillaryOptions.result: PAX8 positive and diffuse box-like CAIX support clear cell RCC.

ancillaryOptions.name: CK7 and KIT
ancillaryOptions.result: Pattern would be used if chromophobe RCC is in the differential.

cases.reportingTarget: Kidney, radical nephrectomy: Clear cell renal cell carcinoma, 5.8 cm, WHO/ISUP grade 2, confined to kidney, margins negative, pT1b.
pitfalls: Do not omit renal sinus or vein assessment.
Do not grade chromophobe RCC by WHO/ISUP criteria.

cases.title: Partial nephrectomy: renal oncocytoma
cases.clinicalHistory: 59-year-old woman with a 2.4 cm well-circumscribed enhancing renal cortical mass.
diagnosticSteps: Recognize nested/tubular oncocytic neoplasm with bland nuclei and supportive architecture.
Separate oncocytoma from chromophobe RCC and hybrid oncocytic tumors.
Report tumor size and margin status for the partial nephrectomy.

ancillaryOptions.name: CK7 and KIT
ancillaryOptions.result: Scattered CK7 staining with KIT positivity supports oncocytoma over chromophobe RCC.

ancillaryOptions.name: CAIX
ancillaryOptions.result: CAIX is not the primary discriminator for oncocytoma versus chromophobe RCC.

cases.reportingTarget: Kidney, partial nephrectomy: Renal oncocytoma, 2.4 cm, completely excised.
pitfalls: Do not overcall focal atypia as carcinoma.
Do not ignore chromophobe RCC if diffuse CK7 and raisinoid nuclei are present.

cases.title: Partial nephrectomy: chromophobe renal cell carcinoma
cases.clinicalHistory: 63-year-old woman with a 3.2 cm renal cortical tumor with oncocytic features on biopsy.

image.caption: Chromophobe renal cell carcinoma, eosinophilic variant.

diagnosticSteps: Recognize chromophobe cytology: pale or eosinophilic cells, irregular nuclear membranes, and perinuclear clearing.
Use CK7/KIT pattern to separate chromophobe RCC from oncocytoma when needed.
Report size, extent, margin status, necrosis, sarcomatoid/rhabdoid features, and pT category.

ancillaryOptions.name: CK7 and KIT
ancillaryOptions.result: Diffuse CK7 with KIT positivity supports chromophobe RCC.

ancillaryOptions.name: CAIX
ancillaryOptions.result: Diffuse box-like CAIX would support clear cell RCC instead.

cases.reportingTarget: Kidney, partial nephrectomy: Chromophobe renal cell carcinoma, 3.2 cm, limited to kidney, margins negative, pT1a.
pitfalls: Do not assign WHO/ISUP grade to chromophobe RCC.
Do not call oncocytoma when diffuse CK7 and chromophobe nuclear features are present.

cases.title: Radical orchiectomy: seminoma
cases.clinicalHistory: 35-year-old man with a 4.1 cm testicular mass and mildly elevated beta-hCG; AFP is normal.

image.caption: Seminoma with uniform clear cells and lymphocyte-rich septa.

diagnosticSteps: Recognize seminoma morphology with uniform clear cells, fibrous septa, and lymphocytes.
Correlate serum markers; elevated AFP excludes pure seminoma.
Report tumor size, rete testis involvement, lymphovascular invasion, spermatic cord/margins, GCNIS, and pT category.

ancillaryOptions.name: OCT4 and CD117
ancillaryOptions.result: OCT4 and CD117 positivity support seminoma.

ancillaryOptions.name: CD30
ancillaryOptions.result: CD30 negativity argues against embryonal carcinoma.

ancillaryOptions.name: AFP immunostain
ancillaryOptions.result: AFP is reserved for suspected yolk sac tumor component.

cases.reportingTarget: Testis, radical orchiectomy: Seminoma, 4.1 cm, confined to testis, lymphovascular invasion not identified, spermatic cord margin negative, GCNIS present, pT1.
pitfalls: Do not sign out pure seminoma if AFP is elevated.
Do not omit GCNIS and margin status.

cases.title: Radical orchiectomy: mixed germ cell tumor
cases.clinicalHistory: 27-year-old man with testicular mass, elevated AFP, and heterogeneous tumor on gross examination.

image.caption: Mixed germ cell tumor with heterogeneous morphology.

diagnosticSteps: Identify more than one germ cell tumor component.
Estimate component percentages because embryonal carcinoma, yolk sac tumor, choriocarcinoma, and teratoma alter management.
Report lymphovascular invasion, rete testis, spermatic cord/margins, GCNIS, and pT category.

ancillaryOptions.name: OCT4 and CD30
ancillaryOptions.result: Supports embryonal carcinoma component where morphology is appropriate.

ancillaryOptions.name: Glypican-3 and AFP
ancillaryOptions.result: Supports yolk sac tumor component.

ancillaryOptions.name: CD117
ancillaryOptions.result: Useful if seminoma component is suspected.

cases.reportingTarget: Testis, radical orchiectomy: Mixed germ cell tumor composed of embryonal carcinoma 60%, yolk sac tumor 25%, and teratoma 15%, 3.6 cm, lymphovascular invasion present, spermatic cord margin negative, GCNIS present, pT2.
pitfalls: Do not report mixed GCT without percentages.
Do not call pure seminoma in a patient with elevated AFP.

cases.title: Testis tumor: postpubertal teratoma component
cases.clinicalHistory: 31-year-old man with a heterogeneous testicular mass; serum markers show elevated AFP before surgery.
diagnosticSteps: Recognize somatic tissue differentiation within a postpubertal germ cell tumor setting.
Search for additional nonseminomatous components and somatic-type malignancy.
Report teratoma percentage because residual teratoma is clinically important after chemotherapy.

ancillaryOptions.name: No ancillary study
ancillaryOptions.result: Teratoma is usually diagnosed morphologically in the correct clinical setting.

ancillaryOptions.name: 12p amplification or i(12p)
ancillaryOptions.result: Supports postpubertal germ cell tumor pathway when the context is uncertain.

cases.reportingTarget: Testis, radical orchiectomy: Mixed germ cell tumor with teratoma component, 40% of tumor; no somatic-type malignancy identified.
pitfalls: Do not call postpubertal teratoma benign.
Do not omit a somatic-type malignant component if present.

cases.title: Radical orchiectomy: spermatocytic tumor
cases.clinicalHistory: 67-year-old man with a slow-growing testicular mass and normal serum AFP and beta-hCG.

image.caption: Spermatocytic tumor with characteristic variable cell size.

diagnosticSteps: Recognize a non-GCNIS-associated germ cell tumor in an older patient.
Separate spermatocytic tumor from seminoma, lymphoma, and embryonal carcinoma.
Search for sarcomatous transformation because it changes prognosis and reporting.

ancillaryOptions.name: OCT4 and CD117
ancillaryOptions.result: Classic seminoma profile is not expected.

ancillaryOptions.name: CD45 and lymphoid markers
ancillaryOptions.result: Use when lymphoma is in the differential.

cases.reportingTarget: Testis, radical orchiectomy: Spermatocytic tumor, 5.2 cm, confined to testis, no sarcomatous transformation identified, spermatic cord margin negative.
pitfalls: Do not call this seminoma.
Do not imply GCNIS-derived mixed GCT pathway without evidence.

cases.title: Testis tumor: Sertoli cell tumor
cases.clinicalHistory: 42-year-old man with a 1.8 cm well-circumscribed testicular mass and normal germ cell tumor markers.

image.caption: Sertoli cell tumor with sex cord-stromal differentiation.

diagnosticSteps: Recognize sex cord-stromal architecture rather than germ cell tumor morphology.
Assess malignant-risk features including size, infiltrative growth, necrosis, cytologic atypia, and mitotic activity.
Use sex cord-stromal markers when morphology overlaps with germ cell or metastatic tumors.

ancillaryOptions.name: SF1, inhibin, calretinin
ancillaryOptions.result: Supports sex cord-stromal differentiation.

ancillaryOptions.name: OCT4 and SALL4
ancillaryOptions.result: Negative staining argues against germ cell tumor.

cases.reportingTarget: Testis, tumor, excision: Sertoli cell tumor, 1.8 cm, no necrosis, no lymphovascular invasion, mitotic activity not increased, margin negative.
pitfalls: Do not apply germ cell tumor component accounting to a pure sex cord-stromal tumor.
Do not omit malignant-risk features.

cases.title: Penile biopsy: high-grade penile intraepithelial neoplasia
cases.clinicalHistory: 58-year-old man with an erythematous glans lesion and no palpable inguinal lymphadenopathy.

image.caption: Penile intraepithelial neoplasia with high-grade atypia.

diagnosticSteps: Recognize high-grade squamous intraepithelial atypia.
Determine whether morphology supports HPV-associated or HPV-independent pathway.
Assess the biopsy base and state whether invasion is identified.

ancillaryOptions.name: p16
ancillaryOptions.result: Block-type p16 supports HPV-associated high-grade PeIN.

ancillaryOptions.name: p53
ancillaryOptions.result: Aberrant p53 pattern may support differentiated HPV-independent PeIN.

cases.reportingTarget: Penis, glans, biopsy: High-grade penile intraepithelial neoplasia, HPV-associated pattern. No invasive carcinoma identified in this biopsy.
pitfalls: Do not imply invasion if the biopsy base is not evaluable.
Do not use p16 as a stand-alone malignancy test.

cases.title: Penectomy: invasive squamous cell carcinoma
cases.clinicalHistory: 64-year-old man with an ulcerated glans mass and clinically negative inguinal nodes.
diagnosticSteps: Confirm destructive stromal invasion by squamous carcinoma.
Assign histologic subtype and grade and assess lymphovascular and perineural invasion.
Report depth/anatomic level of invasion and margin status.

ancillaryOptions.name: p16
ancillaryOptions.result: Block-type p16 supports HPV-associated SCC when morphology is compatible.

ancillaryOptions.name: p40
ancillaryOptions.result: Confirms squamous lineage if poorly differentiated.

cases.reportingTarget: Penis, partial penectomy: Invasive squamous cell carcinoma, usual type, moderately differentiated, invading corpus spongiosum, lymphovascular invasion not identified, margins negative, pT2.
pitfalls: Do not omit anatomic level of invasion.
Do not merge PeIN margin status with invasive carcinoma margin status.

cases.title: Penectomy: verrucous squamous cell carcinoma
cases.clinicalHistory: 71-year-old man with a large exophytic cauliflower-like penile mass.

image.caption: Penile verrucous carcinoma with broad pushing architecture.

diagnosticSteps: Recognize well-differentiated verruciform squamous proliferation with broad pushing interface.
Sample and assess the base to exclude mixed usual-type SCC or destructive invasion.
Report subtype, invasion level, margins, and absence or presence of adverse mixed components.

ancillaryOptions.name: No ancillary study
ancillaryOptions.result: Verrucous carcinoma diagnosis is morphology-led with adequate base sampling.

ancillaryOptions.name: p16
ancillaryOptions.result: Usually not block-positive in classic verrucous carcinoma.

cases.reportingTarget: Penis, partial penectomy: Verrucous squamous cell carcinoma, invading lamina propria with broad pushing border; no usual-type invasive SCC component identified; margins negative.
pitfalls: A superficial biopsy cannot exclude a mixed invasive component.
Do not call verrucous carcinoma without adequate base assessment.

cases.title: Penoscrotal skin biopsy: extramammary Paget disease
cases.clinicalHistory: 73-year-old man with chronic erythematous pruritic penoscrotal plaque.

image.caption: Extramammary Paget disease with intraepidermal Paget cells.

diagnosticSteps: Identify intraepidermal Paget cells with pale cytoplasm and atypical nuclei.
Use immunohistochemistry to support primary cutaneous versus secondary urothelial or gastrointestinal involvement.
State whether dermal invasion is identified in the biopsy.

ancillaryOptions.name: CK7 and GATA3
ancillaryOptions.result: Supports Paget phenotype; interpret with clinical context.

ancillaryOptions.name: Uroplakin and CDX2
ancillaryOptions.result: Helps evaluate secondary urothelial or gastrointestinal involvement.

ancillaryOptions.name: SOX10
ancillaryOptions.result: Negative staining helps exclude melanoma.

cases.reportingTarget: Penoscrotal skin, biopsy: Extramammary Paget disease, intraepidermal. No dermal invasion identified in this biopsy.
pitfalls: Do not omit evaluation for secondary involvement when clinical context requires it.
Do not miss melanoma in the differential.
